segments.title: Master James's Savoyard building network
segments.summary: The opening places Master James of St George in a Savoyard architectural world that shaped major western European castles before his Welsh work.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames James as an engineer whose influence is easier to see in castles than in surviving personal records. The discussion links him to Savoy, Lausanne, and the workshop world around Jean Cotereel, while stressing the uncertainty in medieval documentation. It also points to Vitruvius and master-mason training as part of why a king planning major castles would value him.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces James, his broad castle influence, and the Savoy/Lausanne family setting.
The transcript describes him as a leading medieval architect and connects his skill to Vitruvius and master-mason knowledge.

segments.title: What the sources cannot prove about James
segments.summary: A source-focused section explains why James's father, apprenticeship, wife, and even translated names remain hard to pin down.
segments.body: This watch point is useful for understanding the limits behind confident castle-history narratives. Dr. Clarke walks through the John or Jean Cotereel problem, the difficulty of translated specialist works, and the pension evidence used to infer that James's wife had died before him. The caveat matters because the castles are unusually visible, while the builder's biography is reconstructed from scattered records.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses uncertainty over whether John or Jean Cotereel was James's father or master.
The transcript covers missing family evidence, pension inference, and the possibility that the named figures may be separate relatives.

segments.title: Edward I spots a Savoy castle builder
segments.summary: Edward I's contact with Savoy construction work explains how James moved from continental castle projects toward Wales.
segments.body: The lecture follows Edward's return route from crusade and his family-political contact with Philip of Savoy. James is presented as already involved in several Savoy castle projects, giving Edward a practical demonstration of the kind of builder he needed. The moment shows recruitment through dynastic networks and visible engineering performance rather than a modern hiring process.

evidence.note: The transcript says James had built castles before Wales and was involved while Philip of Savoy was constructing several castles.
The transcript describes Edward recognizing good work and James later appearing in Wales in 1278 as a building coordinator.

segments.title: Master of construction in Wales
segments.summary: James's promotion gave him direct royal authority over the great Welsh castle works at Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, and Beaumaris.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke highlights the administrative step that turned James from a skilled builder into a powerful royal officer. Edward, frustrated with the pace of construction, made him master of the king's works in Wales, with wages and authority that put him over multiple strategic castles. This helps explain why the Ring of Iron appears as an integrated royal building program instead of isolated local projects.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Edward's impatience with construction speed and James becoming master of construction in Wales.
The transcript lists his full control over Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, and Beaumaris and notes name variants in French and Latin.

segments.title: Castle builder as royal military officer
segments.summary: James was trusted not only to design castles but also to hold Harlech and support Edward's Scottish campaigns.
segments.body: This section shows why James should be read as more than a technical specialist. He became constable of Harlech, which made him the king's representative in the castle and implied trust in his ability to fight or manage the fortress. Edward later called him for work connected to Scotland and Stirling, underlining that castle expertise was operational state power.

evidence.note: The transcript says James was appointed constable of Harlech and was considered capable of fighting his castles.
The transcript says James worked at Stirling during the 1304 siege and was called to help Edward in Scotland.

segments.title: Sea-supplied castles as occupation deterrence
segments.summary: The central sea-power argument ties James's castle design to water access, reinforcement, and the deterrent machinery of occupation.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains why James matters to a naval-history study guide: Edward's castles had to survive long enough for sea-linked supply and reinforcement to matter. The castles were engines of occupation and deterrence, but only if their siting and strength made best use of terrain and water access. This connects fortress architecture directly to maritime logistics and political control.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the castles engines of deterrence and occupation dependent on water access for security and supply.
The transcript says Edward needed a master architect to make castles as strong as possible and suited to the ground.

segments.title: Which Ring of Iron castles James controlled
segments.summary: The closing survey separates castles James clearly controlled from sites where his involvement is likely, limited, or uncertain.
segments.body: The final substantive section maps James's role across the Ring of Iron. Dr. Clarke identifies Flint, Rhuddlan, Hawarden, Harlech, Caernarfon, Conwy, and Beaumaris as central to his work, while treating Denbigh, Aberystwyth, and Builth more cautiously. The payoff is a clearer sense of how one master builder could shape a royal system while still facing practical limits of supervision.

evidence.note: The transcript reviews which castles James controlled or influenced and notes limits on what one person could oversee.
The transcript returns to Flint, Rhuddlan, and Hawarden as early fast-built castles and links that speed to Edward's preference.
